name: AI & Data Science Foundations - Sem 1
description: Foundations of AI and Data Science: probability, linear algebra, calculus, data handling, EDA and introductory ML.

theory: Probability quantifies uncertainty. Events, outcomes, and probability measures give a formal way to reason about likelihoods. You'll use discrete and continuous distributions to model real-world phenomena.

Statistics summarizes data using measures like mean, median, variance and uses sampling principles to estimate population parameters from samples. Descriptive statistics provide quick insight while inferential statistics help test hypotheses.

Conditional probability and independence underpin many machine learning models. Bayes' theorem connects prior beliefs with observed evidence and is central to probabilistic modeling and decision-making.

definition.label: Definition
definition.text: Probability and statistics study uncertainty and data: probability models random phenomena; statistics describes, estimates, and infers from data.

examples.label: Coin toss
examples.content: A fair coin has P(heads)=0.5. For two tosses, compute distribution of number of heads using binomial probabilities.

examples.label: Sample mean
examples.content: Given sample [2,4,6,8], the mean is 5 and variance is 5.

content.notes: Practice Bayes and sample calculations; distinguish population vs sample statistics.

examQuestions.question: Compute mean and variance for a small sample.
Explain difference between sample and population variance.

pyqs.question: Find P(A|B) given P(A), P(B|A), P(B).

references: Statistics: Robert S. Witte & John S. Witte
An Introduction to Probability Theory and Its Applications — William Feller

theory: Linear algebra describes linear spaces with vectors and matrices; it provides compact notation for datasets and model parameters. Operations like matrix multiplication express combinations of features and weights.
Eigenvalues and eigenvectors reveal directions of variance and are central to dimensionality reduction methods like PCA. Matrix factorizations (SVD) decompose data into interpretable components.
Understanding vector norms, dot products, orthogonality, and projection helps interpret distances and similarity measures used across algorithms and optimization procedures.

definition.label: Definition
definition.text: Linear algebra studies vectors, matrices, linear transformations, and operations that combine them to represent and manipulate high-dimensional data.

examples.label: Linear system
examples.content: Solve Ax=b for parameters using inversion or least squares when A is not square.

examples.label: PCA use
examples.content: Compute covariance matrix and eigenvectors to reduce dimensionality.

content.notes: Practice small matrix computations and implement PCA on toy data.

examQuestions.question: Compute dot product of small vectors.
Apply PCA to reduce a 3D dataset to 2D.

references: Linear Algebra and Its Applications — Gilbert Strang

theory: Calculus gives tools to quantify change. Derivatives measure instantaneous rates of change; partial derivatives extend the idea to multivariate functions used in loss landscapes.
Gradients are vectors of partial derivatives and indicate the direction of steepest ascent; gradient-based optimization steps follow the negative gradient to minimize loss functions.

Understanding Hessians (second derivatives) and convexity provides insight into convergence behaviour; convex problems have single global minima while nonconvex surfaces may contain many local minima.

definition.label: Definition
definition.text: Calculus studies rates of change (derivatives) and accumulated quantities (integrals); in ML it informs gradient-based learning and optimization.

examples.label: Single-variable derivative
examples.content: If L(w)=w^2, derivative dL/dw=2w; gradient descent with η=0.1 updates w←w−0.2w.

examples.label: Multivariate gradient
examples.content: For MSE L(w)=1/n Σ (y−Xw)^2, gradient is −2/n X^T(y−Xw).

content.notes: Compute gradients symbolically and numerically to validate implementations.

examQuestions.question: Perform one gradient descent update for a simple quadratic.

theory: Raw data is messy: missing values, inconsistent types, and incorrect entries are common. Cleaning transforms raw inputs into analyzable forms and is essential before modeling.
Imputation strategies include mean/mode substitution, model-based imputation, or flagging missingness. Outliers may be errors or valid rare events; decide based on domain context.
Feature scaling (normalization, standardization) and encoding categorical variables (one-hot, ordinal) ensure models behave as expected; many algorithms assume inputs on similar scales.

definition.label: Definition
definition.text: Data cleaning and preprocessing prepare raw datasets by fixing errors, imputing or removing missing values, encoding features, and scaling.

examples.label: Imputation
examples.content: Replace missing ages with median or use regression imputation.

examples.label: Encoding
examples.content: One-hot encode colors {red,green,blue} into binary vectors.

content.notes: Automate preprocessing pipelines and test on holdout data to avoid leakage.

examQuestions.question: Design a preprocessing pipeline for a tabular dataset.

references: Feature Engineering for Machine Learning — Alice Zheng & Amanda Casari

theory: EDA uses visual and numerical summaries to understand distributions, relationships, and anomalies. Histograms, boxplots, scatterplots and correlation matrices are fundamental tools.
Exploration helps generate hypotheses, select features, and decide transformations. Pairwise plots and grouping by categories reveal interactions that simple aggregates might miss.
Effective EDA is iterative: inspect, transform, re-inspect. Visualizations should be clear and reproducible; use notebooks to combine plots with code and narrative.

definition.label: Definition
definition.text: EDA is the process of summarizing and visualizing data to discover structure, spot anomalies, and inform modeling decisions.

examples.label: Histogram
examples.content: Plot student scores to check for skewness and choose transforms.

examples.label: Scatter+color
examples.content: Scatter height vs weight colored by group to reveal patterns.

content.notes: Start simple and avoid overfitting to patterns found during EDA.

examQuestions.question: Design an EDA plan for a dataset.

references: Practical Statistics for Data Scientists — Peter Bruce & Andrew Bruce

theory: Feature engineering creates or transforms raw variables into informative inputs for models. Good features often yield larger performance gains than model tuning.
Common techniques include polynomial features, interaction terms, binning, and aggregation over groups or time windows. Domain knowledge guides meaningful constructions.
Automated feature tools exist, but manual, interpretable features are valuable for debugging and deployment. Always validate engineered features with cross-validation to avoid leakage.

definition.label: Definition
definition.text: Feature engineering is designing and transforming input variables to improve model performance and interpretability.

keyPoints: Use transforms like log or binning

examples.label: Datetime features
examples.content: Extract hour, day-of-week, and holiday flags from timestamps.

examples.label: Aggregation
examples.content: Compute mean past actions per user as a feature.

content.notes: Track feature provenance and ensure reproducibility.

examQuestions.question: Give one feature transform to reduce skew.
Design features for a churn model.

theory: Supervised learning maps inputs to labels using labeled examples. The model learns patterns from data (training) and is judged by its performance on unseen data (generalization).

Loss functions measure disagreement between predictions and labels; training minimizes loss on the training set, while validation tests generalization. Overfitting occurs when the model memorizes training noise.
Bias–variance tradeoff balances model simplicity (bias) and data-fitting flexibility (variance). Cross-validation and regularization are practical tools to control overfitting.

definition.label: Definition
definition.text: Supervised learning trains models using labeled examples to predict outputs for new inputs.

examples.label: Regression vs classification
examples.content: Predict house prices vs spam detection.

examples.label: Train/val/test split
examples.content: Split 70/15/15 to evaluate generalization.

content.notes: Avoid leakage and shuffle/time-split appropriately for temporal data.
references: Pattern Recognition and Machine Learning — Christopher Bishop

theory: Linear regression models a linear relationship between features and a continuous target and is simple, interpretable, and often surprisingly effective.
Regularization (Ridge, Lasso) penalizes large weights to prevent overfitting and can improve generalization, with Lasso also performing feature selection.
Diagnostics like residual plots, R^2, and heteroscedasticity checks help assess fit and guide improvements like transformations or robust regression.

definition.label: Definition
definition.text: Regression predicts a continuous target using input features by fitting parameters that minimize a loss such as MSE.

keyPoints: Lasso adds L1 and induces sparsity

examples.label: OLS example
examples.content: Fit a line to points using least squares.

examples.label: Ridge tuning
examples.content: Pick λ via CV to balance bias and variance.

references: Elements of Statistical Learning — Hastie et al.

theory: Classification assigns discrete labels to inputs. Logistic regression models the probability of class membership using a sigmoid on a linear score.
Decision trees partition feature space using threshold rules and are interpretable; ensemble methods combine trees for improved performance.
Evaluate classifiers with metrics beyond accuracy: precision, recall, F1, and ROC-AUC better capture performance in imbalanced settings.

definition.label: Definition
definition.text: Classification predicts discrete categories for inputs; models estimate class probabilities or direct label assignments.

examples.label: Logistic example
examples.content: Predict churn probability from usage features; threshold at 0.5.

examples.label: Tree example
examples.content: Visualize a small decision tree splitting on age and income.

content.notes: Try simple models first and evaluate with appropriate metrics.

examQuestions.question: Name a metric for imbalanced data.
Compare logistic regression and decision trees.

theory: Model evaluation quantifies how well a model generalizes to unseen data. Use holdout sets, cross-validation, and nested CV for reliable estimates.
Metrics depend on tasks: regression (RMSE, MAE), classification (precision, recall, ROC-AUC). Choose metrics aligned with business objectives.
Hyperparameter tuning must be performed on validation splits to avoid optimistic bias. Use pipelines to ensure preprocessing is applied consistently across folds.

definition.label: Definition
definition.text: Model evaluation uses held-out data and metrics to estimate a model's performance on new examples.

examples.label: K-fold CV
examples.content: 5-fold CV trains on 4 folds and validates on 1, repeating and averaging results.

examples.label: Confusion matrix
examples.content: Compute TP/FP/TN/FN to derive precision and recall.

content.notes: Use time-series CV for temporal data and include preprocessing inside CV pipelines.

examQuestions.question: Design an evaluation plan for a classification task.

references: Practical Machine Learning and scikit-learn docs

theory: Optimization finds model parameters that minimize a loss function. Gradient descent and its variants (SGD, momentum, Adam) are the backbone of modern learning algorithms.

Stochastic methods use minibatches to scale to large datasets and introduce noise that can help escape shallow local minima. Learning rate schedules and adaptive optimizers affect convergence speed and stability.
Practical concerns include initialization, conditioner methods (Adam, RMSProp), and gradient clipping for stability when dealing with exploding gradients in deep networks.

definition.label: Definition
definition.text: Optimization in ML adjusts parameters to minimize loss, commonly via gradient-based methods.

examples.label: Mini-batch SGD
examples.content: Use batch size 32 to compute gradients and update weights iteratively.

examples.label: Learning rate schedule
examples.content: Reduce learning rate on plateau to fine-tune convergence.

content.notes: Monitor training and validation losses to detect divergence or overfitting.

theory: Neural networks stack layers of linear transformations and nonlinear activations to model complex functions. The perceptron is the simplest unit combining inputs linearly and applying a step or smooth activation.

Activation functions (sigmoid, ReLU, tanh) introduce nonlinearity, enabling networks to approximate arbitrary functions with enough capacity. Depth and width trade off representation power and training difficulty.
Training uses backpropagation to compute gradients layer-by-layer and update weights via gradient descent. Proper initialization and activation choice affect gradient flow during training.

definition.label: Definition
definition.text: A neural network is a parameterized function composed of layers of linear operations and nonlinear activations trained to minimize a loss on data.

keyPoints: Neuron computes w^T x + b then activation

examples.label: Perceptron
examples.content: Single-layer perceptron learns linearly separable functions.

examples.label: Small MLP
examples.content: 2-layer MLP with ReLU can model simple nonlinear regression.

content.notes: Visualize losses and activations; check for exploding/vanishing gradients.
